[One-party state](/wiki/One-party_state "One-party state")[[1]](#cite_note-kushtetuta-1) | 1991 | Parliament, by majority[[1]](#cite_note-kushtetuta-1) | Unicameral

[Semi-presidential republic](/wiki/Semi-presidential_system "Semi-presidential system") | 2018 | [Parliament](/wiki/National_Assembly_(Armenia) "National Assembly (Armenia)"), by majority | Unicameral
[Constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") | 2021 | Parliament, by two-thirds majority if there is no joint nomination | Bicameral

is a type of [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") with a [republican](/wiki/Republic "Republic") form of [government](/wiki/Government "Government") in which [political authority](/wiki/Political_authority "Political authority") is entrusted to the [parliament](/wiki/Parliament "Parliament") by multiple [electoral districts](/wiki/Electoral_district "Electoral district") throughout a country. In this system, voters elect members of parliament, who then make [legislative](/wiki/Legislative "Legislative") decisions on behalf of their constituents.

first_paragraph: A **unitary parliamentary republic** is a type of [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") with a [republican](/wiki/Republic "Republic") form of [government](/wiki/Government "Government") in which [political authority](/wiki/Political_authority "Political authority") is entrusted to the [parliament](/wiki/Parliament "Parliament") by multiple [electoral districts](/wiki/Electoral_district "Electoral district") throughout a country. In this system, voters elect members of parliament, who then make [legislative](/wiki/Legislative "Legislative") decisions on behalf of their constituents.

| * [v](/wiki/Template:Basic_forms_of_government "Template:Basic forms of government") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Basic_forms_of_government "Template talk:Basic forms of government") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Basic_forms_of_government "Special:EditPage/Template:Basic forms of government") |

A **unitary parliamentary republic** is a type of [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") with a [republican](/wiki/Republic "Republic") form of [government](/wiki/Government "Government") in which [political authority](/wiki/Political_authority "Political authority") is entrusted to the [parliament](/wiki/Parliament "Parliament") by multiple [electoral districts](/wiki/Electoral_district "Electoral district") throughout a country. In this system, voters elect members of parliament, who then make [legislative](/wiki/Legislative "Legislative") decisions on behalf of their constituents.

The [legislature](/wiki/Legislature "Legislature") in a parliamentary republic may consist of one or more separate [assemblies](/wiki/Deliberative_assembly "Deliberative assembly"), houses, or chambers. This distinction is called a cameral structure and according to it, a republic may be [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameralism "Unicameralism") (just a single assembly), [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism") (two assemblies), or [tricameral](/wiki/Tricameralism "Tricameralism") (three assemblies).

## List of unitary parliamentary republics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitary_parliamentary_republic&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: List of unitary parliamentary republics")]

| Country | Formerly | Parliamentary republic adopted | Head of state elected by | Cameral structure |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Albania](/wiki/Albania "Albania") | [One-party state](/wiki/One-party_state "One-party state")[[1]](#cite_note-kushtetuta-1) | 1991 | Parliament, by majority[[1]](#cite_note-kushtetuta-1) | Unicameral |
| [Armenia](/wiki/Armenia "Armenia") | [Semi-presidential republic](/wiki/Semi-presidential_system "Semi-presidential system") | 2018 | [Parliament](/wiki/National_Assembly_(Armenia) "National Assembly (Armenia)"), by majority | Unicameral |
| [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") | [Semi-presidential republic](/wiki/Semi-presidential_system "Semi-presidential system") | 1991[[note 1]](#cite_note-A-2) | Parliament | Unicameral |
| [Barbados](/wiki/Barbados "Barbados") | [Constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") | 2021 | Parliament, by two-thirds majority if there is no joint nomination | Bicameral |
| [Botswana](/wiki/Botswana "Botswana") | British protectorate ([Bechuanaland Protectorate](/wiki/Bechuanaland_Protectorate "Bechuanaland Protectorate")) | 1966 | Parliament, by majority | Unicameral |
| [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria") [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria") | One-party state | 1989 | [Direct election](/wiki/Direct_election "Direct election"), by second-round system | Unicameral |
| [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia") [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia") | Semi-presidential republic | 2000 | Direct election, by second-round system | Unicameral |
| [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic") | One-party state (part of [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia")) | 1989 (independent since 1993) | Direct election, by second-round system  (since 2013; previously parliament, by majority) | Bicameral |
| [Dominica](/wiki/Dominica "Dominica") | [Associated state](/wiki/Associated_state "Associated state") of the United Kingdom | 1978 | Parliament, by majority | Unicameral |
| [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia") | Occupied by the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") (one party state) | 1918[[note 2]](#cite_note-B-3) | Parliament, by two-thirds majority | Unicameral |
| [Fiji](/wiki/Fiji "Fiji") | Military dictatorship | 2014 | Parliament, by majority | Unicameral |
| [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland") | Semi-presidential republic | 2000[[note 3]](#cite_note-C-4) | Direct election, by second-round system | Unicameral |
| [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(country) "Georgia (country)") | Representative Democracy Governed as a Unitary Parliamentary Republic. | 1921 | Direct election, led by a group of people called “parliaments” | Bicameral |
| [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece") | Military dictatorship; [Constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") | 1975[[note 4]](#cite_note-5) | Parliament, by [supermajority](/wiki/Supermajority "Supermajority")[[note 5]](#cite_note-6) | Unicameral |
| [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") | One-party state | 1990 | Parliament, by absolute majority | Unicameral |
| [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland") | Formerly part of Denmark; Constitutional monarchy | 1944 | Direct election, by first-past-the-post | Unicameral |
| [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland") | Coalition | 1949[[note 6]](#cite_note-E-7) | Direct election, by instant-runoff vote | Bicameral |
| [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") | British Protectorate | 1948 | Parliament, by majority | Unicameral |
| [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") | Constitutional monarchy | 1946 | Parliament, by absolute majority | Bicameral |
| [Kiribati](/wiki/Kiribati "Kiribati") | Protectorate | 1979 | Direct election, by first-past-the-post vote | Unicameral |
| [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia") | Occupied by the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") (one party state) | 1918[[note 7]](#cite_note-F-8) | Parliament | Unicameral |
| [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon") | Protectorate ([French mandate of Lebanon](/wiki/French_mandate_of_Lebanon "French mandate of Lebanon")) | 1941 | Parliament | Unicameral |
| [North Macedonia](/wiki/North_Macedonia "North Macedonia") [North Macedonia](/wiki/Republic_of_Macedonia "Republic of Macedonia") | One-party state (part of [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia "Yugoslavia")) | 1991 | Direct election, by second-round system | Unicameral |
| [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta") | Constitutional monarchy | 1974 | Parliament, by majority | Unicameral |
| [Marshall Islands](/wiki/Marshall_Islands "Marshall Islands") | UN Trust Territory (part of [Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands](/wiki/Trust_Territory_of_the_Pacific_Islands "Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands")) | 1979 | Parliament | Bicameral |
| [Mauritius](/wiki/Mauritius "Mauritius") | Constitutional monarchy | 1992 | Parliament, by majority | Unicameral |
| [Moldova](/wiki/Moldova "Moldova") | Semi-presidential republic | 2001 | Direct election, by second-round system | Unicameral |
| [Montenegro](/wiki/Montenegro "Montenegro") | One-party state  (Part of [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia "Yugoslavia"), and after [Serbia and Montenegro](/wiki/Serbia_and_Montenegro "Serbia and Montenegro")) | 1992 (independent since 2006) | Direct election, by second-round system | Unicameral |
| [Nauru](/wiki/Nauru "Nauru") | Australian Trust Territory | 1968 | Parliament | Unicameral |
| [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") | One-party state | 1989 | Direct election | Bicameral |
| [San Marino](/wiki/San_Marino "San Marino") | [Autocracy](/wiki/Autocracy "Autocracy") (part of the Roman Empire) | 301 | Parliament | Unicameral |
| [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia") | One-party state (part of [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia "Yugoslavia")) | 1991 (independent since 2006) | Direct election, by second-round system | Unicameral |
| [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") | Constitutional monarchy (part of [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia")) | 1965 | Direct election (since 1993) | Unicameral |
| [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia") [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia") | One-party state (part of [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia")) | 1989 (independent since 1993) | Direct election, by second-round system  (since 1999; previously by parliament) | Unicameral |
| [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia") | One-party state (part of [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia")) | 1991 | Direct election, by second-round system | Bicameral |
| [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") | Constitutional monarchy | 1961 | Parliament, by majority | Bicameral |
| [Suriname](/wiki/Suriname "Suriname") | Military dictatorship | 1987 | Parliament, by majority | Unicameral |
| [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") [Republic of China (Taiwan)](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") | [One-party military dictatorship](/wiki/Dang_Guo "Dang Guo") (Mainland) [Constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Taiwan_under_Japanese_rule "Taiwan under Japanese rule") (Taiwan) | 1946[[note 8]](#cite_note-G-9) | [Electoral college](/wiki/National_Assembly_(Republic_of_China) "National Assembly (Republic of China)"), directly elected by the electorate | Tricameral |
| [Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago "Trinidad and Tobago") | Constitutional monarchy | 1976 | Parliament | Bicameral |
| [Togo](/wiki/Togo "Togo") | Presidential republic | 2024 | Parliament | Bicameral |
| [Vanuatu](/wiki/Vanuatu "Vanuatu") | British–French [condominium](/wiki/Condominium_(international_law) "Condominium (international law)") ([New Hebrides](/wiki/New_Hebrides "New Hebrides")) | 1980 | Parliament and regional council presidents, by majority | Unicameral |

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitary_parliamentary_republic&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-A_2-0)** Was previously a parliamentary republic between 1971 and 1975.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-B_3-0)** Estonia was previously a parliamentary republic from 1918 until 1938, when a new constitution with a more presidential system was adopted; the country was occupied and annexed by the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") in 1940-41 (and again in 1944-91) .
3. **[^](#cite_ref-C_4-0)** Formerly a semi-presidential republic, it is now a parliamentary republic according to David Arter, First Chair of Politics at Aberdeen University. In his "Scandinavian Politics Today" (Manchester University Press, revised 2008 [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9780719078538](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9780719078538 "Special:BookSources/9780719078538")), he quotes Nousiainen, Jaakko (June 2001). "From semi-presidentialism to parliamentary government: political and constitutional developments in Finland". *[Scandinavian Political Studies](/wiki/Scandinavian_Political_Studies "Scandinavian Political Studies")*. **24** (2): 95–109. [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.1111/1467-9477.00048](https://doi.org/10.1111%2F1467-9477.00048). as follows: "There are hardly any grounds for the epithet 'semi-presidential'." Arter's own conclusions are only slightly more nuanced: "The adoption of a new constitution on 1 March 2000 meant that Finland was no longer a case of semi-presidential government other than in the minimalist sense of a situation where a popularly elected fixed-term president exists alongside a prime minister and cabinet who are responsible to parliament (Elgie 2004: 317)". According to the Finnish Constitution, the president has no possibility to rule the government without the ministerial approval, and does not have the power to dissolve the parliament under his or her own desire. Finland is actually represented by its prime minister, and not by its president, in the [Council of the Heads of State and Government](/wiki/European_Council "European Council") of the European Union. The 2012 constitution reduced the powers of the president even further.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** Also [between 1924 and 1935](/wiki/Second_Hellenic_Republic "Second Hellenic Republic").
5. **[^](#cite_ref-6)** Supermajority of 67% (two-thirds) required for the first two rounds. If the vote fails, the supermajority threshold is lowered to 60% (three-fifths), then absolute majority, and then simple majority ([plurality](/wiki/Plurality_voting "Plurality voting")). See "Article 32", [*The Constitution of Greece*](https://www.hellenicparliament.gr/UserFiles/f3c70a23-7696-49db-9148-f24dce6a27c8/THE%20CONSTITUTION%20OF%20GREECE.pdf) (PDF) (2019 ed.), Athens: [Hellenic Parliament](/wiki/Hellenic_Parliament "Hellenic Parliament"), 7 June 1975, [archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20240225020309/https://www.hellenicparliament.gr/UserFiles/f3c70a23-7696-49db-9148-f24dce6a27c8/THE%20CONSTITUTION%20OF%20GREECE.pdf) (PDF) from the original on 25 February 2024
6. **[^](#cite_ref-E_7-0)** [Irish head of state from 1922 to 1949](/wiki/Irish_head_of_state_from_1922_to_1949 "Irish head of state from 1922 to 1949").
7. **[^](#cite_ref-F_8-0)** Latvia was previously a parliamentary republic between 1921 and 1934 when the then prime minister Kārlis Ulmanis took power in a *coup d'état*. In June 1940 Latvia was occupied and annexed by the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union").
8. **[^](#cite_ref-G_9-0)** The [Additional Articles of the Constitution of the Republic of China](/wiki/Additional_Articles_of_the_Constitution_of_the_Republic_of_China "Additional Articles of the Constitution of the Republic of China") was passed on May 1, 1991 effectively transforming into a semi-presidential system. The Republic of China government was defeated in the [Chinese Civil War](/wiki/Chinese_Civil_War "Chinese Civil War") in 1949-50 to the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") retreating to the island of Taiwan, which became a de facto one-party state from 1949 to 1987.
